text: We present today a case of a robotic ETAP with hemilateral TAR for a recurrent ventral hernia. CT scan is showed. A moderate size ventral hernia is appreciated. Poor placement is shown in the diagram.
The surgeon enters the retrorectus space using OptiView access, and the left retrorectus space is developed with the use of a Bovie cautery device.

The surgeon uses a robotic system to incise the medial aspect of the left posterior rectus muscle fascia, exposing the midline with diastasis. The right-sided retrorectus fascia becomes visible, and the surgeon performs a crossover by incising the right posterior rectus fascia until the underlying muscle is visualized.

The surgeon identifies the ventral hernia and incises it, carefully dissecting the tissue to achieve an intraperitoneal view. The hernia is completely excised, ensuring no bowel is present within the hernia sac.

The surgeon encounters a moderate-sized defect under tension, preventing closure. A right-side hemicomponent separation is performed by incising the muscle fascia to expose the transversus abdominis muscle, which is then visible superiorly in the operative field.

The surgeon uses a barbed absorbable suture to close the peritoneal defect, which is visible superiorly in the surgical field. The defect appears to be under less tension, facilitating the closure process.

The surgeon demonstrates the final result of the peritoneal closure, showing a tension-free closure. The hernia defect and diastasis are serially closed using an absorbable barbed suture, with the hernia sac being placated until the old defect is completely closed.

The surgeon measures the abdominal cavity, approximately thirty by thirty centimeters, preparing to place a synthetic permanent lightweight mesh. The mesh is laid down laparoscopically through an eight millimeter port.
Fibrin glue is applied to the surgical site, and the pneumoperitoneum is released, marking the end of the procedure.
